dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at 3953 in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Maya, and this is Jordan with city rescue. What made you call 911?
I've been having trouble breathing and left arm weakness for about 30 minutes.
She also has numbness and pain on her left side, especially in her shoulder and leg.
Ma'am, I will ask you a few questions to assess your awareness. Okay?
Okay.
Can you tell me your name?
Barbara.
Barbara, where are you now?
In my bedroom.
And do you know what day it is?
Saturday?
Barbara, can you squeeze my fingers?
Sure.
Right hand grip strong. Left is weak.
Eyes open spontaneously.
Answers are clear and appropriate, no speech issues.
Airway is patent, no obstruction or abnormal sounds.
Breathing is 18, regular, and calm.
Heart rate is 83, strong and regular.

Blood pressure is 165 over 117 right now. 22. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: Barbara, when did the pain on your left side start? 23. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: It started around 5 PM, but it's gotten worse since. 24. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; EMT: Have you felt any numbness or weakness on one side of your body? 25. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs and symptoms; Patient: Yes, mainly on the left, both the arm and leg.
Barbara, do you have any medical conditions or previous injuries?
She had a car accident months ago that caused left-side numbness.

What were you doing around the time the numbness started? 29. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Patient: I was at home just watching a movie. Nothing physically demanding.
Describe the pain in your left shoulder.
It feels heavy and kind of like a cramp.
Does the pain spread to other areas of your body?
It stays in my shoulder, but my left arm and leg are numb.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how severe is the pain?
I'd say an 8.
Barbara, is it harder to breathe right now?
A bit, but not so that I'm straining.
BP is now 116 over 82. It appears to be trending down a bit.
Breathing remains 18 and not labored.
Let's check your blood sugar now.
The meter is showing 0, but we will recheck this soon.
Pulse is now 51 and still regular.

dialogue.role: Assessment of mental status / Short & Long term memory assessment; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Barbara, who am I?
You're Maya.
Nice, and what about that car accident months ago?

dialogue.role: mental status, short and long term memory assessment; Patient
dialogue.utterance: Yes, I had numbness from it. I still have some on the left.

dialogue.role: Gross motor and sensory function; Partner
dialogue.utterance: Barbara, can you move your legs for us? 48. Secondary Assessment; extremities and back exam: Gross motor and sensory function; Patient: I can move the right leg, but not the left.
Left shoulder appears slightly swollen compared to the right.
That swelling has gotten worse with time.

Any difficulty walking or balance problems lately? 52. Secondary Assessment; extremities and back exam: Gross motor and sensory function; Patient: Not that I noticed. I've been walking around on my own.
Just performing oxygen saturation check.
SpO2 is reading 0. Need to recheck later.
Let me prep the leads for the EKG now.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with no elevation or ectopy.
Partner, EKG is normal but we should follow stroke protocol 5.
Agreed. We need IV, 12-lead, and transport to ED.
Preparing to start IV in the right antecubital.
IV started successfully on first attempt in the right AC.
Setting up continuous monitoring now.
Monitor is active with normal readings so far.
Barbara, we need to take you to St. Mary's for imaging.
Go ahead.
Seat belts are on and fastened. Ready for transport.
All systems are safe. Moving now.
St. Mary's, we're transporting a 44-year-old female with left-side weakness.
Performed BEFAST stroke scale assessment with positive results-left facial droop, left arm and leg weakness. 69. Reassessments; retake vital signs: bp; Partner: Rechecking BP - now it is 105 over 69.
Heart rate is now 81 and regular.
Respiration is now 19, still regular and without labored breathing.
Still an 8 on the pain scale? Any changes?
Still 8, ongoing in the left shoulder.
Rechecking blood sugar; it is now 79-within normal limits.
Rechecking EKG. Still sinus rhythm with no changes.
No new findings on EKG. All signs stable.
She has a known history of anxiety but is alert, oriented, and ambulatory upon arrival.
IV established in the right AC, 12-lead EKG obtained. No ST elevation reported.
Preparing for immediate transport to CT scan.
Final checks complete. Ready for transport.
Programming into the system as a priority for acute stroke evaluation.
All data transmitted. I'll update the hospital on any changes during transport.
Starting transport protocols per hospital alert set earlier in the field.
Pulse remains stable at 81 and regular.

Rechecking SpO2. Still reading 0, but this is expected due to equipment or probe placement. 86. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Bystander: She has anxiety as a medical history, though tonight's event does not feel like an anxiety attack as far as she knows.
Still IV monitor showing normal perfusion and no complications yet.
Protocol stroke 5 still in effect, no changes required in this patient based on stats trending.
Letting you know: IV is in right antecubital and taped well. No supplemental oxygen or meds given yet.
EKG stable with no changes since we took it.
Patient still self-aware, aware of the IV, and no complaints of associated pain from the IV.
Transport protocol fully in place and ready.
Have prepared for receiving at ED and will update with updated stats en route.
Ready for transport. All pertinent info sent to the hospital beforehand.
Per hospital protocol, transporting directly to CT as no contraindications.
CT requested and hospital is expecting patient within ten minutes of departure.
Ready for departure. All channels, documentation, and communication accounted for.
Nothing new showing in EKG. No new changes.
No events at this time-patient calm, IV patent, no distress.
Anticipating arrival soon. Any updates will be shared directly.
